project_overview.project_name: Nigeria: COVID-19 Action Recovery and Economic Stimulus Program (CARES)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To expand access to livelihood support and food security services, and grants for poor and vulnerable households and firms
project_overview.beneficiaries: Poor and vulnerable households, farmers, and micro and small enterprises (MSEs) affected by COVID-19 economic crisis; targeting approximately 2.5 million beneficiaries for safety nets, 1.3 million farmers, and 64,000 firms

primary_locations: Specific focus on North East (Borno, Yobe, Adamawa, Taraba)
North West (Kano, Katsina, Jigawa, Sokoto, Kebbi, Zamfara, Kaduna, Nasarawa)
South West (Lagos, Ogun, Oyo, Osun, Ekiti, Ondo)
South South (Delta, Rivers, Bayelsa, Cross River, Akwa Ibom, Edo)
South East (Abia, Anambra, Enugu, Ebonyi, Imo)
North Central (Benue, Kogi, Kwara, Niger, Plateau)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes - partial. The program operates in fragile and conflict-affected areas, particularly the North East (Borno, Yobe, Adamawa, Taraba) experiencing insurgency and banditry. The program includes specific provisions for internally displaced persons (IDPs) and conflict-affected households, particularly in the North East under Results Area 2. Security assessments and management plans are required for affected states.

key_components.component_name: Results Area 1: Increased Social Transfers, Basic Services, and Livelihood Support
key_components.description: Scaling up safety net interventions including state social transfers to aged/disabled/chronically ill/urban poor (DLI 1.1), labor-intensive public works (LIPW) in social services (DLI 1.2), livelihood grants for economically active households (DLI 1.3), and community-driven basic service infrastructure microprojects (DLI 1.4). Implemented through CSDAs, SCTUs, and PWFPIUs.

key_components.component_name: Results Area 2: Increasing Food Security and Safe Functioning of Food Supply Chains
key_components.description: Supporting agricultural inputs and services to farmers (DLI 2.1), improved agricultural infrastructure including irrigation and feeder roads (DLI 2.2), agricultural assets for production and food processing (DLI 2.3), and upgrading water/sanitation in wet markets (DLI 2.4). Implemented through Fadama platform in all 36 states and FCT.

key_components.component_name: Results Area 3: Facilitating Recovery and Enhancing Capabilities of MSEs
key_components.description: MSE matching grants to co-finance new loans (DLI 3.1 - 40% of loan amount), operational support grants for staff salaries and utilities (DLI 3.2), and IT enhancement grants for digital technology adoption (DLI 3.3). Implemented through BOI GEEP or state MSE platforms.

Federal-level coordination, capacity building, M&E, independent verification of DLIs, engagement of verification agents, technical support to states, peer learning facilitation, and financial management. Implemented by Federal CARES Support Unit (FCSU).

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal CARES Support Unit (FCSU) in the Federal Ministry of Finance, Budget and National Planning (FMFBNP), with state-level implementation through State CARES Coordination Units (SCCUs) and delivery platforms (CSDAs, Fadama, MSE agencies)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Three-tier federal structure: Federal CARES Steering Committee (FCSC) for policy direction, Federal CARES Technical Committee (FCTC) for technical oversight, and FCSU for day-to-day management. At state level: State CARES Steering Committee and State CARES Coordination Unit. Coordination with Nigeria Governors Forum (NGF) for peer learning.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: State governments' public procurement systems for PforR component (following state procurement laws); World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF component. Thresholds vary by state but generally: RFQ for <NGN 5 million, NCB for NGN 5-100 million, ICB for >NGN 100 million. Contracts exceeding OPRC thresholds excluded from PforR financing.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Results Framework with 3 PDO indicators and 11 intermediate indicators. Independent Verification Agents (IVAs) verify DLI achievement every 6 months. Third-party monitors (civil society organizations) conduct social accountability monitoring. State Auditor-Generals conduct quarterly audits of beneficiary lists and payments. M&E units at state and federal levels track implementation progress. Annual evaluation studies planned.

identified_risks.risk_description: Nigerian economy highly dependent on oil revenues; COVID-19 and oil price collapse have severely constrained government revenues. Risk of continued economic contraction, reduced fiscal space, and inability to maintain program funding flows to states.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Performance-based disbursement incentivizes state governments to provide budgetary resources; SFTAS program supports states to protect social expenditure; adoption of fund release policy for improved predictability of resource flows; World Bank support for macroeconomic stability through CoFFER DPF

identified_risks.risk_description: Multiple MDAs with overlapping mandates in social protection, agriculture, and MSE support create coordination challenges. Absence of comprehensive shock-responsive strategy integrating all sectors. Uneven capacity across states.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establishment of State CARES Coordination Units to centralize coordination; use of existing well-performing platforms (CSDP, Fadama, GEEP) with proven track records; flexibility for states to select DLIs based on priorities; federal technical support through FCSU; peer learning through NGF

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak internal controls over payroll and potential for fraud in beneficiary selection and misuse of grants. Risk of ghost workers in payroll systems. Weak capacity in internal audit functions. Budget execution performance historically low (average <40%).
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Beneficiary selection from verified social registers (SSR, SRB, URB); quarterly audits by State Auditor-Generals; grants disbursed in tranches upon work completion; biometric capture and BVN linkage to payroll; independent verification by IVAs; GRM for complaints; anti-corruption protocol with EFCC/ICPC; enhanced accountability framework for soft expenditures; SFTAS program incentivizes improved PFM

identified_risks.risk_description: Rehabilitation and upgrading of infrastructure may cause localized environmental impacts (pollution, waste management, GHG emissions). Risk of exclusion of vulnerable groups, elite capture, SEA/SH, GBV/IPV. Weak E&S management capacity in states. Contextual risks from climate change, insurgency, banditry, and farmer-herder conflicts.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: E&S screening of all activities; voluntary land donation protocol with informed consent; exclusion list for high-risk activities; capacity building for state E&S staff; GRM strengthening; GBV response teams in states; community management committees; security assessments and management plans; climate-smart agricultural practices; E&S audits every 6 months; engagement of E&S specialists at federal and state levels

identified_risks.risk_description: Political interference in procurement and beneficiary selection; weak governance and reform implementation challenges; recent nationwide protests and civil unrest with vandalism of government offices.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strict application of World Bank Procurement Regulations; transparent procurement processes with competitive bidding; use of digital payment systems for transfers; independent verification of results; third-party monitoring by civil society; anti-corruption framework with EFCC/ICPC; GRM for grievances; regular World Bank supervision; support for government offices and officials through insurance provisions

identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of multi-sectoral program across 36 states with varying capacity; short implementation period (24 months) for emergency response; need for rapid scaling up of interventions.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of existing proven platforms with track records; flexibility for states to select DLIs; strong federal coordination through FCSU; intensive capacity building and technical support; hands-on implementation support from World Bank; quarterly review meetings; peer learning facilitation; simplified procedures for emergency response

identified_risks.risk_description: Uneven capacity across states and implementing agencies; weak M&E systems; limited experience with PforR instrument in some states; staff turnover and retention challenges.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Capacity building program for M&E staff; training on operations manuals and guidelines; technical support from national platforms (NFCO, GEEP/BOI, NASSCO); engagement of experienced federal officers as coordinators; continuous engagement to discourage arbitrary redeployment of trained staff; World Bank implementation support missions

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of second wave of COVID-19 pandemic leading to renewed lockdowns and movement restrictions. Widespread civil protests and sporadic violence. Insurgency and banditry in North East and North West. Farmer-herder conflicts. Climate change impacts.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Program designed with COVID-19 protocols and guidelines for implementation; many interventions can proceed even during lockdowns; security assessments and management plans for affected states; community-level conflict resolution committees; climate-smart agricultural practices; flexible implementation arrangements; provisions for technology-based remote supervision; insurance for program workers and contractors

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: Substantial E&S risk rating (not a standard IPF category, but PforR uses core principles approach)
triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5) - Not currently relevant as no involuntary resettlement envisaged, but voluntary land donation protocol required
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Central GRM at state level leveraging existing structures from CSDA, NASSP, SCTU, Fadama, and GEEP. GRM to be strengthened and formalized. Beneficiaries sensitized on availability and contact information at local, LGA, and state levels. Complaints can be submitted through multiple channels. Redress may include information clarification, payment regularization, registry updates. Third-party monitors and civil society organizations provide additional accountability. World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available for affected communities.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender focus throughout program design: (1) Results Area 1: female outreach facilitators, gender-specific quotas in community plans, female staff for GRM, women-focused business training; (2) Results Area 2: women farmer field facilitators, targeting women farmers for improved seeds, water design for women's multiple uses, assets reducing women's labor drudgery, women prioritized for market cleaning/fumigation jobs; (3) Results Area 3: higher weights for women-owned MSEs in vetting, women agents for beneficiary screening, women entrepreneurs prioritized for grants. All indicators disaggregated by gender with specific targets for female beneficiaries.
vulnerable_groups: Internally displaced persons (IDPs) - particularly in North East

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Program explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity: (1) Operates in fragile and conflict-affected areas (North East, North West); (2) Includes specific support for IDPs and conflict-affected households through Results Area 2; (3) Community-level conflict resolution committees to address herder-farmer-water user conflicts; (4) Security assessments and management plans required for affected states; (5) Private security support included in operational grants for MSEs in FCV states; (6) GBV response teams and SEA/SH prevention measures; (7) Flexible implementation arrangements to accommodate security challenges; (8) Technology-based remote supervision where needed; (9) Insurance provisions for program workers and contractors in high-risk areas
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Nigeria classified as fragile state with multiple fragility factors: (1) Insurgency and banditry in North East (Borno, Yobe, Adamawa, Taraba) and North West (Kano, Katsina, Jigawa, Sokoto, Kebbi, Zamfara, Kaduna, Nasarawa); (2) Farmer-herder conflicts over water and grazing rights, exacerbated by climate change; (3) Weak state capacity and governance challenges; (4) High poverty rates (40% nationally, 70% in North East); (5) Food insecurity affecting 25.6 million people pre-COVID, worsening with pandemic; (6) Youth unemployment (27% nationally) and underemployment (29%); (7) Climate change impacts including droughts, floods, desertification; (8) Recent nationwide civil protests with violence and vandalism; (9) Weak public sector capacity and service delivery; (10) Limited fiscal resources due to oil dependence
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security is a major implementation consideration: (1) Comprehensive security assessment required before implementation in each state, especially North East and North West; (2) Security management plans to be developed and constantly updated; (3) Risk of kidnapping of program workers and contractors documented; (4) Private security support included in operational grants for MSEs in FCV states; (5) Insurance provisions for program workers and contractors; (6) Technology-based remote supervision and monitoring where physical access constrained; (7) Provisions in procurement documents for technology deployment to forestall delays from lockdowns/curfews; (8) Community-level conflict resolution committees; (9) Coordination with state governments on security issues; (10) Flexibility in implementation arrangements to accommodate security challenges
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - partial. Program includes specific provisions for internally displaced persons (IDPs): (1) IDPs targeted through Results Area 1 social transfers using Unified Register of Beneficiaries (URB) in North East states; (2) Results Area 2 includes support for livelihood restoration of conflict-affected households, particularly in North East; (3) State Register of IDPs used for beneficiary selection; (4) Special categorical transfers for IDPs under social protection component; (5) However, program explicitly excludes activities involving physical displacement or resettlement - any land selected for microprojects involving displacement/resettlement is ineligible; (6) Voluntary land donation protocol required with screening to ensure no coercion or displacement; (7) No large-scale land acquisition envisaged; (8) Program aims to restore livelihoods of conflict-affected populations and support their reintegration
